result:
The morning light through my window is not a single beam, but a thousand dancing motes of dust. It falls on the open notebook, illuminating a sentence I can no longer decipher, written in the urgency of 3 AM. Outside, a world hums. A distant lawnmower brays its suburban song. Someone, somewhere, is laughing. The coffee in my mug has gone from scalding to tepid, a quiet betrayal of time’s steady drip.

I think of a conversation I had years ago, with a man on a park bench who spoke of rivers. “We always say a river *flows*,” he’d said, whittling a stick with a small knife. “But the water that passes your ankle is never the same water twice. The river isn’t flowing; the water is being replaced. The river… the river just *is*. A shape that holds the constant leaving.” I never saw him again, but his words return at odd moments, like now, watching the dust particles swirl and flee from the sun’s path.

My mind is not a straight line. It is a meadow full of rabbit holes. One thought of the river man leads to the feel of cold, smooth stones in a stream in Colorado, decades ago. The shock of the meltwater, the sound that was less a sound and more a feeling in the bones. That leads to my father, silent and focused, teaching me to skip a flat stone. He is gone now, and the specific weight of his hand on my small shoulder is a memory I can feel but not quite grasp, like a shape in thick fog. It is astonishing, the quiet absences that populate a life. We are all haunted, not by ghosts, but by the precise textures of moments that have dissolved.

I consider the 1000 words. They are a finite pasture. I could fill them with facts—the boiling point of mercury, the capital of Uzbekistan, the number of breaths an average human takes in a day. But facts feel like stones in the pocket today. Solid, but heavy. I am more interested in the spaces between things. The pause between the flash of lightning and the thunder’s rumble, which is not silence but a held breath. The almost-imperceptible glance between two strangers on a train that contains whole novels of understanding and conjecture. The way, just before sleep, your thoughts unravel into nonsense that feels more true than any logic.

We live in a chorus of tiny apocalypses. Every second, a version of you dies, replaced by a version who has lived one second longer. The cells in your body are in a state of constant, meticulous rebellion—dying, sloughing off, regenerating. You are a castle whose bricks are being replaced one by one as you stand living in it. We call this “being,” but it is a furious, beautiful kind of dying. The river just *is*.

The light has shifted. The motes of dust have settled or moved to another stage. My coffee is now fully cold. I take a sip anyway, and the bitterness is acute, clarifying. There is a fly buzzing against the windowpane, a tiny, persistent engine of life, convinced there is a world just beyond the invisible barrier. I could open the window. But I don’t. I watch its struggle, its brief, frantic orbits. I am, in this moment, the god of this fly’s universe—capable of granting freedom, yet remaining still. The moral weight of this small power is negligible and yet infinite.

Perhaps this is all we can do: pay attention. To witness the dance of dust, the flight of the fly, the memory of a cold stream and a warm hand. To stitch together the ephemeral with the threads of words, which are themselves just breaths given shape. To build a raft of consciousness and float it on the river of replacement, knowing the raft is also part of the flow.

I think of all the hands that have ever held a pen, a chisel, a brush, a trowel. All the voices that have ever sung a lullaby or shouted in anger or whispered a secret into the dark. An immense, silent cathedral of human experience, most of it lost, unrecorded, dissolved back into the hum. My 1000 words are a single leaf falling in that forest. It will land without a sound. But for a moment, it caught the light as it fell.

The notebook page remains. The sentence from 3 AM is still a mystery. Perhaps it is better that way. Some things are not meant to be solved, only held. The fly has ceased its buzzing; it sits on the sill, cleaning its iridescent wings with an air of philosophical resignation. Or perhaps I am projecting. We are all projecting—casting the movie of our meaning onto the blank screen of the world.

I have used most of my words. The pasture is nearly grazed. Soon, this act of reaching into the humming static of my mind and pulling out these particular shapes will be over. They will exist apart from me, these words. They will be a river I have stepped out of. And the thinking, feeling creature I am in this instant—the one tasting cold coffee, feeling the morning sun warm the back of her neck, remembering a park bench and a river’s truth—will be gone, replaced by the next version, who will read this and wonder who exactly wrote it.

The last words are a breath. An acknowledgment. A small, deliberate pebble skipped across the vast, dark water. It sinks, as it must. But for a few hops, it danced in the sun.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
